coredata.dc:title: A novel metal-free nanomaterial P-CN/BC/NCDs preparation and its performance of photocatalytic degradation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xiaoling
Wen, Meng
Guo, Qi
Wang, Gang
Hao, Pengcheng
Liu, Wanyi
Zhan, Haijuan
Chen, Xiaoyan
Li, Heping
coredata.dc:description: The development of photocatalysts with high charge separation and migration efficiencies for environmental remediation using sunlight had been a research priority. In this study, a ternary composite photocatalyst, P-CN/BC/NCDs, was successfully synthesized by thermal condensation and hydrothermal methods, incorporating graphitic carbon nitride (P-CN), biochar (BC), and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs). Alizarin red S (ARS) was selected as the model pollutant to evaluate the photocatalytic degradation performance. P-CN/BC/NCDs exhibited enhanced photocatalytic degradation performance under visible light irradiation, with a 4.5-fold improvement compared to P-CN alone. The optimally NCDs-loaded P-CN/BC nanocomposites exhibited high visible light absorption and high specific surface area. The increased photocatalytic activity was further confirmed by the increase in photocurrent intensity and the decrease in fluorescence intensity and resistance. XPS and FT-IR tests showed that NCDs, as co-catalysts of P-CN/BC, effectively promoted charge separation through ether bonds and electrostatic interactions. It was experimentally verified by free radical trapping experiments and EPR tests that •O2 − was the primary active species in the photocatalytic process, while •OH served as an auxiliary site during the degradation process. Cyclic experiments demonstrated high reusability and excellent stability, with an activity exceeding 93.8 %. Decomposition intermediates and reaction pathways were identified by liquid-quality analysis. Photocatalyst pervasiveness was evaluated by using different pollutants including methyl orange (MO), rhodamine B (Rh B) under similar conditions. This design concept of functional synergistic modification of P-CN materials holds promise for application in various fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Graphitic carbon nitride
Biochar
Nanocomposites
Photocatalytic degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of P-CN, P-CN/BC and P-CN/BC/NCDs catalysts. (b) Partially enlarged view of part a (2θ = 25–30°). (c) FTIR patterns of P-CN, P-CN/BC and P-CN/BC/NCDs catalysts. (d) Partially enlarged view of part a (λ = 1000-2000 cm−1).
SEM images of (a) P-CN/BC, and (d) P-CN/BC/NCDs. (b-c)HR-TEM images of P-CN/BC, and (e–f) P-CN/BC/NCDs and (g) EDS images of P-CN/BC/NCDs.
Nitrogen sorption isotherms of (a) P-CN, (b)P-CN/BC, and (c) P-CN/BC/NCDs.
XPS spectra of the P-CN, P-CN/BC and P-CN/BC/NCDs (a) C 1s, (b)N 1s, (c)O 1s.

Photoelectric test spectra of P-CN, P-CN/BC and P-CN/BC/NCDs catalysts: (a) UV–vis DRS spectra, (b) estimated band gaps, (c) XPS-VB spectra, (d) Fluorescence emission spectra (PL), (e) I-t diagrams, (f) EIS spectra under light.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of ARS using BC, P-CN, P-CN/BC-2 (2 %), P-CN/BC-3 (3 %), P-CN/BC-4 (4 %) and P-CN/BC-5 (5 %) (50 mg catalyst with 50 mg/L ARS under visible light), (b) Photocatalytic degradation of ARS using P-CN, P-CN/BC (3 %), P-CN/BC/NCDs-0.5 (0.5 %), P-CN/BC/NCDs-1 (1 %), P-CN/BC/NCDs-2 (2 %) and P-CN/BC/NCDs-3 (3 %) (50 mg catalyst with 50 mg/L ARS under visible light), (c) Photocatalytic degradation of ARS using BC, P-CN, P-CN/BC (3 %), P-CN/NCDs (2 %) and P-CN/BC/NCDs (2 %) (50 mg catalyst with 50 mg/L ARS under visible light), (d) Recycling experiment, (e) XRD spectra of P-CN/BC/NCDs before and after used, (e) FT-IR spectra of P-CN/BC/NCDs before and after used.

Analysis of the photocatalytic process of P-CN/BC/NCDs catalysts: (a–c) EPR test results for•O2 −,•OH and h+ at different times (0, 5, and 10 min), (d) Degradation ofcontaminants with the addition of different radical scavengers, (e) Possible mechanism of the photocatalytic processes.
Proposed ARS degradation pathway using P-CN/BC/NCDs under visible light irradiation.
Schematic diagram of the preparation process of P-CN/BC/NCDs.
BET and its pore size and pore capacity for P-CN, BC, P-CN/BC and P-CN/BC/NCDs.

head.ce:title: A novel metal-free nanomaterial P-CN/BC/NCDs preparation and its performance of photocatalytic degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of High-efficiency Utilization of Coal and Green Chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ningxia University, Yinchuan, 750021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of High-efficiency Utilization of Coal and Green Chemical Engineering
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of High-efficiency Utilization of Coal and Green Chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ningxia University, Yinchuan 750021, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Baotou Research Institute of Rare Earth, Baotou, 014030, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Baotou Research Institute of Rare Earth

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Baotou Research Institute of Rare Earth, Baotou, 014030, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of photocatalysts with high charge separation and migration efficiencies for environmental remediation using sunlight had been a research priority. In this study, a ternary composite photocatalyst, P-CN/BC/NCDs, was successfully synthesized by thermal condensation and hydrothermal methods, incorporating graphitic carbon nitride (P-CN), biochar (BC), and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs). Alizarin red S (ARS) was selected as the model pollutant to evaluate the photocatalytic degradation performance. P-CN/BC/NCDs exhibited enhanced photocatalytic degradation performance under visible light irradiation, with a 4.5-fold improvement compared to P-CN alone. The optimally NCDs-loaded P-CN/BC nanocomposites exhibited high visible light absorption and high specific surface area. The increased photocatalytic activity was further confirmed by the increase in photocurrent intensity and the decrease in fluorescence intensity and resistance. XPS and FT-IR tests showed that NCDs, as co-catalysts of P-CN/BC, effectively promoted charge separation through ether bonds and electrostatic interactions. It was experimentally verified by free radical trapping experiments and EPR tests that •O2 − was the primary active species in the photocatalytic process, while •OH served as an auxiliary site during the degradation process. Cyclic experiments demonstrated high reusability and excellent stability, with an activity exceeding 93.8 %. Decomposition intermediates and reaction pathways were identified by liquid-quality analysis. Photocatalyst pervasiveness was evaluated by using different pollutants including methyl orange (MO), rhodamine B (Rh B) under similar conditions. This design concept of functional synergistic modification of P-CN materials holds promise for application in various fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The need for clean water is one of the greatest challenges facing humanity in the 21st century. Semiconductor photocatalysis has attracted attention as an advanced oxidation process (AOP) for the removal of chemical pollutants from water [1]. Graphitic carbon nitride (CN), as a representative non-metal photocatalytic material, has been widely employed in hydrogen production via water splitting [2,3], pollutant degradation [4–6], photocatalytic CO2 reduction [7,8], and other solar energy photovoltaic conversions [9–12]. However, its application is still limited by rapid electron-hole recombination, narrow visible light absorption range, and low conductivity. To overcome low catalytic activity of CN [13–15], various improvement strategies have been proposed, including pore enlargement [16], morphology and crystal modification [17], doping induction [18], plasma deposition [19], heterostructure construction [20], dye sensitization [21] and organic modification [22]. Among these, the structural modulation of CN through non-metal doping to enhance photocatalytic activity has attracted considerable attention [3,23]. Considering the environmental concerns, the development of environmentally friendly and efficient photocatalytic materials has become a pressing issue.

With the characteristics of large specific surface area, abundant active functional groups, multiple active adsorption sites, wide source of raw materials, low cost, and environmentally friendly, biomass carbon materials are considered as one of the most promising photocatalyst carriers [24–27]. As such, waste biomass resources are ideal for the production of highly efficient photocatalysts, in line with China's principles of carbon neutrality and carbon peaking. Meanwhile, it can also effectively reduce the complexation of photogenerated electrons and holes by accelerating the transfer of electrons from the photocatalyst surface [28,29]. It can be compounded with conventional photocatalysts such as TiO2 and CN to efficiently remove various pollutants through adsorption-catalysis synergy [30]. Xiao et al. successfully prepared BC/CN composites to get rid of formaldehyde by using discarded lobster shells as raw materials through a thermal shrinkage process, demonstrating a significant synergistic effect between BC and CN [31]. Tang et al. modulated the electronic structure of CN by providing electron-withdrawing groups via moss biochar for photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics via persulfate activation [32]. However, the electron transfer at the interface of the BC/CN heterojunctions remains unexplored. Furthermore, these binary materials exhibit gradual and slow degradation of target pollutants [33]. Therefore, our research aims to environmentally modify BC-modified CN binary composites and enhance their photocatalytic degradation efficiency. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have shown promising applications in photocatalysis due to their size-dependent properties, good conductivity, and upconversion luminescence [31,34]. Nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCDs) exhibit enhanced light absorption and excellent activity. The introduction of nitrogen can regulate the energy barriers in electron transfer within CQDs, thereby accelerating the rate of electron migration. Furthermore, NCDs have been widely used to construct low-cost, metal-free CN-based photocatalysts due to their abundant precursors. Jian et al. constructed CQDs/CN nanocomposites with excellent electron transfer properties [35]. Zhao et al. prepared CQDs-modified tubular CN, which exhibited excellent carbamazepine degradation properties [36]. The ultimate goal of this study was to perform secondary modification of P-CN/BC composite materials to mitigate the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, improve electron transfer rate, and enhance photocatalytic degradation efficiency.

Additionally, in this study, a novel ternary composite material was developed by creatively utilizing nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) as co-catalysts in combination with the binary composite material P-CN/BC, using a simple hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). BC was extracted as precursor material from the abundant medicinal residue of Astragalus in the region. The successful synthesis of the P-CN/BC/NCDs material was confirmed through comprehensive characterization of its morphological structure and photoelectric properties. The photocatalytic activity of the ternary composite material was evaluated by degrading the water pollutant Alizarin red S (ARS). The experimental results revealed that the prepared P-CN/BC/NCDs ternary composite exhibited a degradation efficiency for ARS that was 4.5 times higher than that of P-CN and 14 times higher than that of traditional CN. Furthermore, this study provides a detailed description of the structural properties and photocatalytic pathways of the composite material, offering a promising avenue for future research in the field of photocatalysis.

A total of 10 g (79.2 mM) of melamine was placed in a 500 mL beaker equipped with a magnetic stirrer. Slowly added 3 mL (54 mM) of sulfuric acid to the mixture and stirred for 10 h at room temperature. After the reaction was completed, the mixture was filtered, washed, and dried and the resulting solid was transferred to the crucible and calcinated in a muffle furnace. The crucible was heated at a rate of 2 °C/min to 350 °C and held for 1 h. Then, it was heated at a rate of 5 °C/min to 520 °C and maintained for 2 h. After natural cooling, the sample P-CN (light yellow) was obtained.

Astragalus root was washed with water until a neutral pH was reached and then dried. The dried material was crushed and sieved to obtain different size distributions (200 mesh). The obtained powder was placed in a quartz boat, transferred to a tube furnace, purged with N2, and heated to 700 °C, holding for 2 h. After cooling, black powder biochar was prepared and referred to as BC.

The P-CN/BC composite material was prepared using a one-step co-pyrolysis method. P-CN and different amounts of biochar (BC) were mixed in specific ratios (BC masses of 0 %, 2 %, 3 %, 4 %, and 5 % of P-CN mass). After grinding the mixture, it was transferred to a crucible, which was then placed in a tube furnace. N2 was purged, and the furnace was heated to 550 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min, holding for 4 h. After cooling, the binary composite photocatalyst P-CN/BC was prepared.

An amount of citric acid and urea (ncitric acid: nurea = 1) was dissolved in water. After stirring, the mixture was placed in an autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 4 h. The mixture was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. The resulting dark brown solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. NCDs was obtained by ultrafiltration membrane filtration of the solution and freeze-drying.

The P-CN/BC/NCDs ternary composite material was prepared using a hydrothermal method. In short, 1 g of P-CN/BC (P-CN/BC-3) and a certain amount of NCDs (NCDs masses of 0 %, 0.5 %, 1 %, 2 %, 3 %, and 4 % of P-CN/BC mass) were taken, stirred for 10 min, and then transferred to a high-pressure autoclave. The reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling, filtration, and drying, the ternary composite photocatalyst P-CN/BC/NCDs was prepared.

Samples were taken on an XRD ray diffractometer (D/MARX2200/PC, Rigaku, Japan) using XRD patterns obtained in the 2θ range using Cu-Kα radiation. FT-IR spectra were recorded using FTIR spectroscopy (TENSOR II, Bruker, Germany) at wave numbers from 400 to 4000 cm−1. The chemical state was analyzed using an American-made XPS instrument (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM, Zeiss Sigma 300, Germany) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, Thermo Fisher Talos F200X, USA) were used to observe the morphology of the samples. The instrument used for the determination of nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and specific surface area was the Specific Surface Area and Pore Size Analyser (Microcrystallography, ASAP 24603, USA). The photoluminescence (PL) was measured on a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7100).

50 mg of catalyst was dispersed in 50 mL of contaminant solution (ARS, 50 mg/L, MO, 50 mg/L, TC, 50 mg/L, Rh B, 20 mg/L, MB, 20 mg/L, AF, 20 mg/L) and stirred until adsorption equilibrium. After switching on the light, samples were taken every 15 min and measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency E was calculated as follows:
E =
(
1 −
C C
)
× 100 %
where C
0 was the initial concentration and C was the concentration of the solution at the time of sampling.

The crystal structure and chemical composition of the samples were characterized. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 1 a) revealed two distinct peaks at 13° and 27° for P-CN, P-CN/BC, and P-CN/BC/NCDs, corresponding to the (100) and (002) crystal planes of P-CN [37]. The (100) peak was typically attributed to the surface filling of the triazine ring structure, while the (002) peak was associated with the interlayer stacking structure of co-blocked aromatic rings [37,38]. The results indicated that the intensity of the (002) diffraction peak was weakened in P-CN/BC and P-CN/BC/NCDs, suggesting the disruption of long-range order and the generation of defects in P-CN. Both P-CN/BC and P-CN/BC/NCDs exhibited a gradual shift of the peaks towards higher angles. The interlayer spacing decreases (from 0.168 nm to 0.167 nm–0.166 nm), indicating that the introduction of NCD disrupts the lattice structure of the P-CN/BC material [39–41]. The FT-IR spectra characterization of different samples was shown in Fig. 1c. The main structure of CN was retained (Fig. S1). In the case of P-CN/BC, the peak intensities at 1245-1639 cm−1 were weakened, and a red-shift was observed. This suggested that the carboxyl groups on the BC may undergo structural changes or removal upon interaction with P-CN. The absorption peaks at 1200-1700 cm−1 in the spectrum of P-CN/BC/NCDs were significantly weakened and red-shifted. This might be due to the doping of NCDs and the strong interfacial supramolecular interactions (charge and electrostatic interactions) between P-CN/BC, which regulated the electronic structure [21,42].

Pure P-CN material exhibited a layered structure with micropore aggregation (Fig. S2). The introduction of BC resulted in a more porous structure for P-CN/BC (Fig. 2 a). SEM-EDS elemental mapping confirmed the distribution of elements, indicating the composition of P-CN and BC in the composite (Fig. S3b). Further observation through TEM of P-CN/BC revealed the presence of numerous biochar particles (average particle size of approximately 24 nm) attached to the layered P-CN structure (Fig. 2b). The HRTEM image of P-CN/BC (Fig. 2c) was found with a lattice spacing observed in the composite material was 0.321 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of P-CN, and 0.680 nm (Fig. S4), corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of P-CN [9,43]. Additionally, a lattice spacing of 0.520 nm was observed, corresponding to the crystal surface of BC material attached to P-CN (Fig. 1c). To further verify this, HRTEM of BC was separately performed (Fig. S4), confirming that the newly observed lattice fringes belong to BC itself. This confirmed the successful formation of the P-CN/BC composite. With the addition of more NCDs, the surface structure of P-CN/BC/NCDs material became rougher (Fig. 2d) and was covered by a large number of spherical particles (average particle size of approximately 2 nm). The lattice spacing d = 0.231 nm observed in the TEM image (Fig. 2f) corresponded to the (110) crystal lattice plane of NCDs, indicating the successful coordination of NCDs [44]. EDS elemental mapping (Fig. S3c) showed a distribution of C, N, and O elements. Elemental analysis (EA) revealed a higher percentage of carbon (C) in the binary material P-CN/BC compared to P-CN, while the percentage of nitrogen (N) increased in the ternary material P-CN/BC/NCDs. EA and EDS analyses (Table S2 and Table S3) demonstrated consistent trends in element content, indicating that the composite material is not limited to the surface.

The chemical composition and bonding environment of the prepared catalyst were characterized using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). The analysis results were shown in Fig. S6, where C, N, and O were identified as the major elements in these samples. Fig. 4 a displayed the C 1s spectra, which could be divided into three peaks centered at 284.8 eV, 285.7 eV, and 288.3 eV, typically attributed to sp2 hybridized C=C bond or surface hydrocarbon species, C-OH species involved in carbon dioxide absorption, and sp2 hybridized carbon in the triazine ring, respectively [46]. In particular, the position of sp2 hybridized carbon in P-CN/BC shifted slightly from 288.18 eV to 288.31 eV, indicating the possible embedding of BC into the CN framework, forming interactions between BC carbon and P-CN [38]. Furthermore, compared to P-CN, P-CN/BC exhibited a decrease in the C=C content from 25.4 % to 23.7 %, while the N-C=N content increased from 69.3 % to 72.5 %, possibly due to the conversion of C=C (sp2) and N-C=C to N=C-N. The increased degree of conjugation was favorable for electron delocalization and might enhance the photocatalytic capability of P-CN/BC, consistented with the results of infrared tests [33,47]. Fig. 4b presented the convoluted N 1s spectra with four peaks corresponding to sp2 hybridized pyridinic nitrogen (C=N-C) at 398.7 eV, sp3 hybridized pyrrolic nitrogen (C-NH-C) at 400 eV, sp3 hybridized graphitic nitrogen (N-C3) at 400.8 eV, and charge effects or π-excitations on the heterocycle at 404.2 eV [33,47,48]. With the addition of BC, the percentage of C=N-C in P-CN/BC decreased slightly from 70.6 % to 65 %, while the percentage of N-(C)3 increased from 16.4 % to 17.5 %, indicating an increase in the polymerization degree of the composite material [26]. The N 1s peak of P-CN/BC/NCDs exhibited slight shifts at 398.7 eV, 399.5 eV, and 401 eV, suggesting that NCDs might be loaded onto the surface of P-CN/BC through the formation of C-N=C, N ≡ C, and N-H bonds. Additionally, in the O 1s spectra, the peak around 532.4 eV (Fig. 4c) was likely attributed to the absorption of carbon dioxide and water molecules [33]. In the O 1s spectrum of P-CN/BC, a new peak appeared at 533.7 eV, possibly corresponding to the C-O-C bond, indicating the connection between BC and P-CN through ether bonds and π-π interactions [33,47,49]. With the introduction of NCDs, the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) revealed C-O (carboxyl) at 533.7 eV and C-OH at 532.2 eV [11].

The UV–vis absorption spectra reflected the optical absorption characteristics of all samples. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the intrinsic absorption limit of P-CN was around 444 nm, which corresponded to the π-π peak of the P-CN conjugated system [22]. The intrinsic absorption of P-CN/BC showed a significant red-shift [33]. The intrinsic absorption limit of P-CN/BC/NCDs was at 524 nm, covering the entire visible light range, which was attributed to the electronic bridging effect and unique upconversion effect caused by NCDs [32]. The spectral red-shift indicated enhanced light response and absorption of the material, suggesting that P-CN/BC/NCDs possessed higher visible light responsivity and improved photocatalytic activity in the presence of BC and NCDs [15]. The band structures of P-CN, P-CN/BC, and P-CN/BC/NCDs were studied using the Tauc plot obtained from the transformed UV–vis absorption spectra. The band gap of the catalysts could be calculated from the relationship between (αhν)2 and the photon energy (hν) (Fig. 5b). With the increased in BC content, the semiconductor band gap narrows, which might be attributed to the strong interaction between BC and P-CN [33,47]. Further doping with NCDs results in a band gap of 2.57 eV for P-CN/BC/NCDs [33,47]. The reduction in band gap width indicated that electron transition could occur with less energy, which was favorable for enhancing photocatalytic performance. The results indicated that the P-CN/BC/NCDs catalyst exhibited the strongest light absorption ability. The XPS valence band (XPS-VB) spectra of the samples were measured using XPS (Fig. 5c), and the band structure of the photocatalyst was studied (Fig. S7). According to the analysis, the VB value of P-CN/BC/NCDs was 2.08 eV, which was 0.64 eV higher than that of P-CN (1.44 eV). Generally, a higher VB of a catalytic material indicated a stronger oxidative-reductive ability for the generation of photogenerated holes (h+) and electrons (e−), leading to the generation of more photogenerated electrons [18,20]. Therefore, P-CN/BC/NCDs had a narrower band gap and better light-absorbing ability, which was conducive to improving the catalytic activity of the photoreaction.

The separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs was evaluated using PL spectroscopy (Fig. 5d). With the addition of BC and NCDs, the peak centre of P-CN was blue-shifted and the peak intensity decreased. BC had higher electron transferring capabilities [33]. The introduction of BC could effectively inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and extended the carrier lifetime [33,47]. NCDs served as energy transfer elements in the design of photocatalysts to convert visible and near-infrared light into shorter wavelengths. NCDs as electronic traps could effectively inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs, allowing more opportunities for P-CN/BC/NCDs to participate in photocatalytic reactions [50]. Fig. 5e showed the I-t curves of the three representative samples, with P-CN/BC/NCDs exhibiting the highest photocurrent. The increased in photocurrent intensity indicated that doped BC and NCDs might combine with P-CN in the form of chemical bonds, thereby improving the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers [31,33]. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra of the materials were shown in Fig. 5f, with P-CN/BC/NCDs exhibiting the lowest electrochemical impedance. Compared to the other two samples, P-CN/BC/NCDs had a small charge transfer resistance at the interface, which was favorable for catalytic reactions [49,50]. In summary, the doping of BC and NCDs improved the electronic structure and interface electron transfer of the P-CN precursor, enhancing the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. The photocurrent of the three samples was well reproduced through periodic applications, confirming their stable photoelectric properties. Similarly, the ternary material P-CN/BC/NCDs demonstrated strong light absorption capability and rapid photogenerated carriers photogenerated carriers separation efficiency, playing a crucial role in improving its photocatalytic degradation performance.

To evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalysts, Alizarin red S (ARS) was initially chosen as model pollutant. The degradation assessments were conducted under simulated visible light conditions (450 W metal halide lamp, λ > 400 nm). After determining the optimal concentration and catalyst amounts, the BC and NCDs doping ratios were screened for doping according to Fig. 6 a and b, respectively. As shown by the experimental comparison in Fig. 6c, the best adsorption of ARS was achieved by doping BC (BC: 20.4 %, P-CN: 19.2 %, P-CN/BC: 23.8 %, P-CN/NCDs: 19.8 %, P-CN/BC/NCDs: 24.6 %). This was because the active adsorption sites, oxygen-containing functional groups, and unique mesoporous structure of BC gave the composites good wettability and a certain degree of hydrophilicity, which played a crucial role in the adsorption of dyes in water [33,51]. With continued light exposure, the highest photocatalytic degradation of ARS by P-CN/BC/NCDs was observed(BC: 2.5 %, P-CN: 56.2 %, P-CN/BC: 87.4 %, P-CN/NCDs: 64.4 %, P-CN/BC/NCDs: 100 %). Serving as electron traps, the NCDs could promote interfacial electron transfer from the bulk CN to the NCDs sites and mediate further catalytic reactions. Further, the upconversion photoluminescent properties of NCDs enabled the composites to exhibit enhanced visible light absorption and photocatalytic properties [52]. The pseudo-first-order kinetic equation ln(C
0
/C) = kt was used for the linear fitting analysis of the photocatalytic degradation process (Fig. S8). The rate constants (k) and relative coefficients (R2) for all samples were listed in Table S5. The computational results showed that the reaction rate of P-CN/BC/NCDs to ARS was 0.0478 min−1, which was 4.5 times higher than that of P-CN and 14 times higher than that of conventional CN. These results were consistent with those of previous structural characterization and photoelectrochemical tests. The moderate amount of NCDs increased visible light absorption, thereby exciting more photo-induced electron-hole pairs, which was favorable for photocatalysis. These results were also in agreement with the UV–vis DRS analysis.

A similar trend was observed for the adsorption of P-CN/BC/NCDs to methylene blue (MB), methyl orange (MO), rhodamine B (Rh B), tetracycline (TC), and acid fuchsin (AF) in degradation experiments (Fig. S9). A kinetic curve was shown in (Fig. S8d), and the P-CN/BC/NCDs exhibited higher degradation efficiencies compared with P-CN and P-CN/NCDs (Table S5). The sequence of rate constants for the degradation of P-CN/BC/NCDs was TC (0.02803 min−1) > AF (0.02382 min−1) > MO (0.01986 min−1) > Rh B (0.01704 min−1) > MB (0.0061 min−1). The results suggested that optimal P-CN/BC/NCDs could effectively degrade various toxic pollutants in the aqueous environment. In summary, P-CN/BC/NCDs could effectively reduce organic pollutants under visible light. In conclusion, compared with other g-C3N4 materials (Table S6), the ternary composite catalysts P-CN/BC/NCDs showed the advantages of structural stability, strong catalytic performance, high recovery, and good degradability. By cyclic photocatalytic degradation of ARS under visible light irradiation, the reusability of P-CN/BC/NCDs was investigated. According to Fig. 6d, the degradation rate of P-CN/BC/NCDs was 93.8 % after 4 cycles. This experimental result further verified the above results. It showed that the ternary catalyst possessed high stability in practical applications.

To analyze the stability of P-CN/BC/NCDs, characterization was performed before and after used. From Fig. 6e, the XRD patterns of P-CN/BC/NCD were not significantly different on the characteristic peaks before and after cycling. It indicated that the catalyst was stable and could be continuously purified by visible light regeneration. From the FT-IR (Fig. 6f) spectra, a decrease in the peak intensity in the range of 1200–1700 cm−1 indicated the depletion of functional groups such as C=O carboxyl groups, C=C bonds (aromatic ring double bonds) and imine-containing carbon-nitrogen groups. The structural stability and reusability of the ternary composite catalyst was further demonstrated by these characterisations.

Free radical scavenging experiments were conducted to identify the active species generated during the photocatalytic degradation of ARS by P-CN/BC/NCDs. In this study, IPA, EDTA-2Na, and BQ were used as quenching agents for hydroxyl radicals (•OH), holes (h+), and superoxide radicals (•O2 −), respectively. When EDTA-2Na, IPA, and BQ were added, the photodegradation efficiency of ARS decreased from 100 % to 98.5 %, 82.6 %, and 24.4 %, respectively.

In Fig. 7 a–c, DMPO was used as a spin trapping agent for testing •OH and •O2 −, and TEMPO was used as a spin trapping agent for testing h+. In the EPR experiments, no background peaks were observed during the detection of •O2 − and •OH, but background peaks appeared during the detection of h+. In the dark state, the EPR spectrum of TEMPO showed three signal peaks with a relative intensity ratio of 1:1:1, which were the background peaks of the trapping agent. When illuminated, TEMPO showed a slight decrease in peak signal in the presence of P-CN/BC/NCDs catalysts, indicating that the material produces relatively few photogenerated holes under light conditions. From Fig. 7a, it could be seen that the P-CN/BC/NCDs material can produce •O2 − under light, as evidenced by the characteristic peaks produced by the catalyst under light conditions. Fig. 7b revealed that P-CN/BC/NCDs material could generate •OH under illumination, as evidenced by the four-line EPR spectrum obtained with a relative intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 for the catalyst. From Fig. 7c, it could be seen that the ternary material produced a little of h+ in the light. As shown in Fig. 7d, it indicated that superoxide radicals (•O2 −) were identified as the primary active species in the reaction, followed by hydroxyl radicals (•OH), while the contribution of holes (h+) was minor. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was employed to confirm the involvement of •O2 − and •OH as the major contributing factors in the degradation process. This was consistent with the results of free radical trapping experiments.

Based on the results of EPR spectroscopy and active species trapping experiments, and considering the band structure of the catalyst, a plausible mechanism for the photocatalytic reduction and degradation of ARS by P-CN/BC/NCDs was proposed. Firstly, the doping of BC and NCDs significantly enhanced the adsorption capacity of P-CN towards ARS, which was beneficial for subsequent photocatalytic reactions [8,39,45,47]. It could be seen from Fig. 5(b and c) that the band gap of P-CN/BC was 2.69 eV. Correspondingly, the potentials of EVB and ECB were 1.88 eV (vs NHE) and −0.81 eV (vs NHE), respectively. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy level of NCDs was 2.17 eV (vs NHE). From the UV–vis DRS absorption spectrum of the NCDs solution, the band gap energy was about 2.64 eV [53]. Therefore, the lowest unknown molecular orbital (LUMO) position was located at −0.41 eV (vs NHE). However, the doping of NCDs in P-CN/BC significantly changed the energy band. The measured values of EVB and ECB of P-CN/BC/NCDs were 2.08 eV (vs NHE) and −0.49 eV (vs NHE), respectively. Under illumination, the P-CN/BC/NCDs composite material could absorb more visible light photons with hν ≥ Eg and excite electrons to the CB, generating holes (h+) in the valence band (VB). Meanwhile, BC could effectively collect electrons transferred from P-CN, leaving behind a large number of holes in the valence band of P-CN. The interface contact between BC and P-CN hindered the recombination of electron-hole pairs, ensuring efficient charge separation. Subsequently, the electrons transferred to the surface of BC react with O2, generating •O2 −. The absorption range of visible light was broadened with the introduction of NCDs, which served as electron acceptors and electron mediators that could bind to surface functional groups via π-π conjugation. Therefore, after illumination, NCDs accepted the excited-state electrons from P-CN in BC [54]. Simultaneously, the photo-generated electrons from P-CN were transferred through BC to NCDs. Since NCDs contained carboxyl and pyridine groups, it resulted in higher affinity of P-CN/BC. It facilitated the transfer of electrons from PCN/BC to NCDs and slowed down the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Subsequently, the electrons on the surface of NCDs react with dissolved oxygen in the water medium, producing •O2 −. In summary, the non-metallic materials BCs and NCDs enhanced the electronic excitation and reduced the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs through their synergistic effects, which effectively broadened the optical absorption range of the photocatalysts, and ultimately achieved the effective use of solar energy.
(1) hν + P-CN/BC/NCDs → h+ + e−
(2) O2 + e− → •O2 −
(3) H++•O2 − → •OOH
(4) H++ e-+ •OOH → H2O2
(5) e− + H2O2 → OH− + •OH
(6) h+ +H2O → •OH + H+
(7) •OH + •O2 − + ARS → Degradation products

Toxicity of degradation products formed by photodegradation of toxic pollutants in water was an important parameter. The degradation products were analyzed in this paper using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS). There were two possible paths for ARS degradation, as shown in Fig. 8 . The free radical •O2 − oxidises the ARS dye and the main intermediates of this degradation pathway were P1 (m/z = 342), P2 (m/z = 196), P3 (m/z = 166), which were similar to those reported in the literature [55,56]. When ARS was attacked by •OH at the C-5 position, it would be degraded through the formation of the intermediate P4 (m/z = 298) and P5 (m/z = 258). Finally, the intermediates were converted to non-toxic small molecules such as CO2, SO4 2− and other mineralized small molecules. Moreover, the TOC of ARS-degraded solutions on P-CN/BC/NCDs composites was investigated. The TOC removal rate reached about 68.9 %, indicating that the ARS was capable of both degradation and mineralisation (Fig. S11). However, the TOC removal was 31.1 % lower than the degradation efficiency due to the presence of small amounts of aromatic residual compounds.

In summary, the residual astragalus waste in the region had been effectively utilised as a source of BC, and the ternary composite photocatalysts P-CN/BC/NCDs were successfully prepared by thermal condensation and hydrothermal methods. The experimental results demonstrated that BC, serving as electron bridges, and NCDs, acting as electron acceptors, synergistically improved the charge distribution in the P-CN material and provided more active sites. P-CN/BC/NCDs exhibited a larger specific surface area, narrow band gap, expanded spectral absorption range, and high efficiency in the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. P-CN/BC/NCDs showed excellent catalytic activity in the degradation of water pollutants such as Alizarin Red S (ARS), Rhodamine B (Rh B), Methyl Orange (MO), and Acid Fuchsin (AF), with ARS degradation rate 4.5 times higher than that of P-CN. The catalytic performance of the catalyst remained stable after four cycles, with degradation rates still exceeding 93.8 %. Furthermore, free radical scavenging experiments and EPR tests also confirmed that •O2 − was the primary active species in the photocatalytic degradation process under illumination. This work provided new ideas for the preparation of carbon-based materials with enhanced photocatalytic properties using herbal wastes in a greener, more environmentally friendly and efficient manner.

Xiaoling Liu: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Meng Wen: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Qi Guo: Investigation, Data curation. Gang Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Pengcheng Hao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Wanyi Liu: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Haijuan Zhan: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Xiaoyan Chen: Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Heping Li: Methodology, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
